Okay, same with all of our other big clients, and the reason being is we want to avoid Fatigue. Okay, this is the reason that we do. A monthly for the warm comparatively to a cold. Okay. Now, moral of the story. This is very important to understand. We need to. Insure. That we differentiate these two. Okay. In terms of the content style, and what's covered within the webinar. What is the same is if you're doing affluent demographics or if you're selling to the general public, those are still the same. Okay, those are always the same. There's no difference in the framework. Just to be clear on what I'm talking about there, by framework, again, I'm communicating to the master webinars SOP. Which is... Again, we'll be shared with this video here. It's also in our master SOP list. So by framework, I mean like these kind of things. And we dive deep into each one of those. What differentiates is what you talk about. In the content, and how the frame is just a little different. With cold audiences, they have no idea who you are. So they don't care about you at all. They're very quick and judgmental. And they're going to leave right away if they don't get something out of it, damn near immediately. OK, due to the fact that cold audiences don't give a fuck about you at all. We have to immediately jump into content. We don't get the luxury of being able to just sit there and start yapping. One of the mistakes that people make is they see the word 'immediate,' okay? And they think that I am exaggerating. They think that they have the time. To like, Oh, hey, guys, like, welcome into the to the webinar. It's so good to see you. Go ahead and drop in the chat. Like, where are you guys from? You know, where are you guys tapping into? How's everybody's day? You know, everybody having a good day? Drop a one in the chat if you're having a good day. And. Long story short. Wee. Do not get the luxury of doing that to cold audiences. OK, so when we talk about cold specifically. You have to jump. straight into it. Okay, and I mean literally. Straight into it. If they miss... The first couple minutes. like some of y'all are right now. Пока. Okay. Fuck them. That's the kind of mentality you want to have with a cold audience versus what a lot of people do is the opposite. And that's generally why they don't jump immediately into things. What they're trying to do is— they are trying to. Ensure that they get like as big of a warm audience live that they can. And at that point, that and only then do they generally start. That's not how code works. So as an example, if I started the webinar and I was talking about something that relates to, like, maybe I'm going to sell some people into the inner circle, okay, and I do an entire webinar on, like, show rate. As soon as the webinar starts, I'm going to be like, 'What's going on, everybody?' Welcome in. My name is Jeremy Haynes. I'm going to go through no-name client examples that you guys have never heard of. And I'm going to show you how some people were struggling with, like, 30% show rates and got them up to 70. This is the biggest misconception that people have. So I'll give you a great example of this. Just recently. We had one of our clients. Pretty big name. You know, has a substantial organic audience. And we retarget the hell out of it every time that we do a webinar. And for a lot of the past webinars that we've done for this client, they've really just only done the monthly warm live webinar. So, by 'warm,' just for our audience perspective, website visitors, all the social interaction. Outside of the social interaction, and just to be clear on what I mean by that, like people who visit the profile, people who engage with a post, people who press on a button on an ad, people who message the page, people who save the post, those different audience options. Video viewers to ideally high video view thresholds, like 25% plus is an example. And for shorter videos, ideally 75% plus. Or at least 50. customer lists, email lists, okay? And because their size of those lists is so substantial, they've never really had to go beyond it. They've started to spend upwards of $200,000 a month on their warm audience. And to be quite honest, they still have a lot of room to continue to scale into that. Like let's, let's work in at least one of those a month as a test. And let's do $100,000 budget on it. And let's see how it goes. Let's get our feet wet.' So we set up a cold webinar. We spend $100,000 on completely cold audiences. And by cold, it's also very important to note. I am completely excluding all of those warm audiences I just described. Because even if I set up totally cold audiences and I don't add those exclusions, cold audiences can still be warm audiences. Cold nowadays pretty much goes after whoever the fuck it wants. So if you're going to do a true cold audience test, you have to exclude the warm audiences in their entirety. OK, so moral of the story is I. wants to ensure it's true cold. And I want to make sure I treat them like they have no idea who I am and they don't give a fuck about me yet. Okay? So we get all these people to show up to this client's webinar. And by the way, it was a cheaper cost than warm. We're getting about $6 cost per webinar registration. Okay, so we got a ton of people to sign up for this. And we had a pretty good show rate too. We actually averaged about a 45% show rate for a cold audience, which was much higher than I generally set the expectation. Usually with cold in terms of the show rate. We'll do about a 15% show rate on average is what we generally will tell clients it is. We try not to set the expectation higher than that. It's not that it can't be higher than that. But... Okay. How do you break down that barrier? As I described. You have to instant fucking taneously jump into something that has some good value density to it. It's okay to do a little intro. But like I described, the intro can't be soft. You have to have like things that people genuinely give a fuck about. Okay, cold audiences. Are generally very like Result-driven. And you know, need I not say? You have to have Some type of outcome. That they're going to be able to generate off of this. So. Your content. Can't just be framing. Okay, you actually want to try to avoid framing content. So. A lot of the historical best practices for webinars. Have been to have This is kind of... Not like a jab at Russell Brunson. You know, I appreciate Russell Brunson. I respect Russell Brunson. But like, he was the one who perpetuated this idea more than almost anybody else. He would say to go into a webinar and have like three secrets, you know, and he would try to talk about like the vehicle framing and all these like little framing tactics. And a lot of those things, not only do they not work now, they're going to have your webinar. Have such an extreme drop-off rate so, like, if you look at your retention of the webinar. And, you know, over here represents the start and over here represents the end. And it just looks like this. Like, you have. A steep drop off in the beginning and then, like, you retain the people who stay till the end. That is mainly due to. Again, a terrible intro, no value density, nothing being result oriented, no outcome content. And you did a lot of framing. In almost all instances, that's what causes problems at the beginning of the beginnings of these things. There is a certain point. Kind of as I demonstrate in this visual here, where people will stick regardless. But again, you're not there to sell to a minority of the people who show up. You're there to ideally get the people who show up to stick. OK, so in comparison, usually a nice like. Webinar retention graph looks like this. You're still going to get drop off, but it won't be nearly as extreme. OK, the other big mistake that most people make. Real quick. For anybody here who does YouTube, okay, and you look at your YouTube analytics, when you go into your YouTube analytics or anybody here who has a BSL as an example. When you go look at those analytics, you can see that most people will drop off after a few minutes. So it's like, I'll sometimes, as an example, make like 45-minute videos or like an hour-long video. And it'll say the average watch time is about six to eight minutes. Okay? When does that therefore... What does that statistic represent in terms of when I should start my pitch or add a pitch? Okay. For me, as an example, I don't just pitch at the end. Okay. I like to pitch multiple times throughout the cold webinars. Okay. So as an example, I usually have three different instances of a pitch. Because if I pitched solely here at the end, I'd be pretty fucked in terms of my revenue potential. Because I'm pitching to the smallest quantity of people that are left at the webinar at that point if I exclusively pitch at the end. Okay. What I like to do. And again, this applies to YouTube. This applies to BSLs. I like to structure the webinar in a way. Where it's almost like a mini webinar. For that first third, that we're talking about here. And then I go deeper. Into the topics in the second third. And then I go the deepest. Into that. Final third. Okay, with a little pitch. The pitch gets more aggressive at the end. We can be more pitchy at the end. Dude, it's just fucking giving people information at that point. It's like. Hey guys, what's going on? Welcome in. My name is Jeremy Haynes. I'm going to get your show right up today. We're going to be talking about. how to take show rates from as low as like 30 to 50 percent. And by the way, if you're in that bracket, you're doing awful. I'm going to show you how we average usually all the way upwards of like 70%. Sometimes our outliers even get all the way up to 80% nowadays. Which currently is not normal. It used to be normal. It's not as normal now. We're going to be going through my hammer them strategy. We're going to be going through some confirmation page best practices. We're going to be going through some information related to value-dense emails. We're going to be talking about trust assets and sales assets. And without further ado, we're going to dive right into it. If your show rate currently struggles, and this applies for webinars, this applies for call funnels. This can even apply for a Hydra campaign where you're leveraging lead forms and you're just trying to get a better connection rate. The main reason that you are struggling is because you are likely talking to people who are too early in the sales process. With too low of an interest level. Okay? All people. who you are selling to. have an interest level that is quantifiable. Your interest level of your salespeople is obviously the highest. They're the most convinced that a deal should get done. The interest of a lead when they first come in is very low, comparatively. The interest of a lead can come in typically on four stages— is what we like to call it. Curious? General interest, very interested and convicted. Okay. Those are the four levels that we generally think about when we're talking about show rate and we're talking about interest levels. Most people, for you, if you're dealing with a 30 to 50% show rate, by the time the webinar comes, by the time the call happens, they're still in the curious bracket. And curious is not enough to get people to prioritize talking to you in comparison to all the other things they have to do in a day. So the game of getting show rate up is as simple as increasing the interest level of the lead before that key event comes up that they need to show up for. Do me a favor, drop a one in the chat if that makes sense. Okay, now here's what we're going to talk about. Again, we're talking about the hammer them strategy. We're going to talk about confirmation page best practices, value-dense emails, and then trust assets and sales assets. Let's get started with the hammer them strategy. You know, the guy got me like 50% of the way there. And there's very clearly still like another 50% of the stuff I need. OK, so the key when you officially start talking about like the value-dense part in the webinar is what I continue to use the word 'edging' for, okay? I need to edge the people. I need to give them enough. to get them really close to the point where they can do it successfully and they can take a shot at it and they can even potentially see some results from doing it the way that I articulated. But I need them to also feel, while they're doing it, that there's still some clear questions that are left unanswered. I need them to hit some kind of struggles when they start to do it on their own. That didn't lead them to the conclusion of, it makes total sense to buy that guy's stuff. Because. even if I could do it on my own. The other demographic is. They hear everything you're saying and they realize, like, wow, I could do that on my own. But like, why would I? It just kind of makes sense to hire this guy to help me. It seems like it'd be easier if he helped me and it would save me some time. So you're going to have the people who do stuff. Those are the people who are going to buy from you later. OK, and you're going to have the people who listen to what you're saying and they think to themselves. Honestly, dude, it just makes a lot more sense to just. just go and and buy it. You know, seems like it'd be easier. If it costs more than $3,500, push them to a call. Stomar says, 'Dropping price that early in the pitch? Yeah, absolutely. Absolutely. There's never an instance where I'm not dropping the price.' Okay. Specifically for affluent audiences. I'm not wasting their time. I'm dropping the price straight away. If you're selling to the general public, that's the only time where you might withhold the price. I would still pitch the price to the general public at the end. Like in that last third of the pitch. However, in the first third and the second third pitch, I might withhold the price unless I get questions about it in the chat. At that point, I'm going to happily talk about it. I am not in any way, shape, or form against pitching people with the price. I do not believe in any way, shape, or form in the logic that those of you who try to withhold price participate with. You like to believe that if you withhold the price, you'll get more people to book a call. And although that's true, I don't load my calendar or my client's calendars up with turds. Okay, not gonna happen because we're not putting people into debt. If you do financing options, that's great. That's a strong argument to get people on calls. We're not putting people in debt. You either got the money or you don't. So it's very important that we disclosed it. Okay, so anyway. Generally, after each pitch for the first third, the second third, and the final third, we're going to do a Q &A. Okay. So right after we pitch and we get done, we're going to say real quick: anybody who has questions, if you'd like an opportunity to unmute yourself and ask, just raise your hand. Happily on mute. And if not, just drop your question in the chat. We can answer anything that we've talked about here. Now, here's what generally happens, especially in the first third and the second third. You're still going to talk about essentially the exact same shit that you just spoke about in the first third. Just in more depth. And the final third, you might give some examples of the things that you've talked about in the second third and the first third. Okay. Point being. I can do a little Q &A that generally opens up a segue to just go right back into the content. You want to keep things smooth. Okay. These these webinars should be smooth, shouldn't be choppy, shouldn't be hard. You know, it should be really easy. And almost all these questions are going to lead to an opportunity to just get right back into the flow. Remember, cold audiences. Okay, everything's going to come back to this. They don't give a fuck about you. Okay. They are cruel. So. Even though you're answering questions, the questions that are getting asked might not pertain to what the people who are still there care about. It might be something super, super particular. That only that individual person cares about. A great example of this is like, sometimes I'll get comments on my YouTube channel from some dipshit who's like, 'Hey, can you do a video that goes through like this extremely tactical specific thing?' And I'm like, 'Of fucking course not.' I'm absolutely not going to talk about that. It pertains to literally nobody but you. Okay, that's not what a webinar is for. A webinar is for talking to everybody who's there. You see, it's every person who's there is who I'm talking to. I'm not talking to an individual that has a specific question. So if somebody has a question in the chat. And that question does not make sense to answer for everybody else. We're not fucking talking about. You see what I'm saying? OK, so anyway, we got one more question from Timothy. He says, 'Where do you draw the line between the general public and affluent? At what point do you consider someone affluent versus general public? Pretty much anybody who makes $150,000 or more. We've seen time and time again that they have the ability to purchase products as high as $15,000 to $25,000. Generally, anybody who makes less than $150,000 a year is lumped into the general population, the general, and the general public— as an audience. So again, pretty much anybody with $150,000 or more in annual income. Although I'd still consider that very impoverished. We see them time and time again, typically through debt, and be able to purchase higher-ticket products.' İyi günler dilerim. Ben. Okay, anyway, back to the flow. So, the moral of the story. And this is very important— you understand this because I want to make this really clear. In these segments of the pitch. Okay, in these segments of the webinar. It's a very simple flow. Value-dense content. Followed by the pitch. Followed by Q &A. And then it's a cycle from there. And you just repeat that process. Okay. You essentially go through that flow. Three times. The only part of this that changes is when you finally get to the final third. Okay. What you're doing is. You have a lot more time on the pitch in the final third. and you have as much time as you need on the Q &A. But the first third and the second third, those pitches are two to five minutes. And the Q &As are ideally two to five minutes. Okay, the final third. You can fucking yap as much as you want while you're pitching. Okay. Do I ask permission to pitch every time? No. Great question, Jose. No. I'm only asking permission to pitch the first time. I'm only asking permission to pitch that first time. The second time, I'm just rolling right into it. And it's kind of just an opportunity. They already gave me permission. Right. Imagine if you kiss a girl that you just meet and you ask her permission to do it. You're like, 'Hey, mind if I give you a quick smooth, you know?' Nowadays, you got to ask permission to do that. You can't just do it. She says, 'Yeah, go ahead.' It's like, 'Do you really have to ask the second or third time? If it's within that same window of time, kind of be awkward if you did.' You know, same thing here. We don't want to create awkwardness. No need to ask a second or third time. You just do it. Okay, so long story short. Once we get that flow down right here, we go value dense content, pitch, Q &A. Okay. And I just get right back into the value dense content. Again, there's one other visual that I want to make sure I described to you. Okay. Which is. the density. Okay. how deep we go. So I'll visualize it like this for you. Kind of like an upside-down funnel. It's like during the first third. It's more surface-level. And as the webinar progresses. I get deeper and deeper with the examples I'm providing. So it's like I got my first section. my second section and my final third section there. So obviously in the first section of the webinar, I could talk about the things they need to do. but I can't go like super in depth into it, but I have to work through it all. Okay. Then I pitch. Then I do Q &A. Naturally, in that Q &A, there will be questions since I only went surface level. That's going to lead to my segue into my second part. My second part, I'm going deeper. So instead of just talking about the hammer them strategy as an example for my fake webinar and improving show rates, that second part. I might go into an ad account. And I might show them like some content or like on my TikTok. In my saved videos folder, I have so much great hammer them content examples. I've just accumulated there. Same thing with Instagram. They're great. I will sit there. Hold that up. Go through examples of. And the second part as well. I'm going to go through, like, some confirmation page examples. I'm going to go through. I'm going to go through some email examples. I'm going to go through some trust and sales asset examples. Then I'm going to pitch again. Again, you'll notice. There's a great opportunity there to pitch a little differently. For the second pitch and then the third pitch as well. So in the second pitch, the reason I can pitch it a little differently is because now I've gone deeper. You see? So now I can reiterate. I can anchor back to the first pitch. So I can be like, 'Look, guys, I've already talked to you about. The offer that I have for you. And I think it makes a lot of sense.' I think it's really easy for you to get to the point where you fuck me quickly. And the value shifts into your favor. Very quick. And you'll notice, with all these examples that I've provided, now that we've gone deeper into the subject. That having a little bit of help and giving you all the examples that I have inside of this offer I have for you, in addition to the handholding that you're going to get, can just make it all go a little faster. That'd be the point of purchasing this, is to not only become... fully aware, because you guys got to remember, right? It's a webinar to be transparent and honest with you. I'm holding back, right? I'm putting you on game. I think everybody here can agree that I'm giving you guys a lot of information, but at the same time, I'm withholding information that's only inside of the paid program that you guys can get access to. Okay, you have more than enough now to go out and take action on this, but this is your only training material. Inside of the actual product or service that I can sell to you. We'll either hold your hand to do it, give you all the information you need to do it on your own, but we'll just support you to get the result faster. That's why you'd purchase at this point. That's why you would take this action by pressing this link in the chat— is because you want to buy speed. Okay. So for those of you who are here and you want to buy speed at this— the link is in the description, the link is over here, go press that button, get your call booked, and let's get you what you're after. Let's increase the speed of you getting that result, let's increase the support that you get throughout the process of executing this, because at the end of the day, people talk about wasting time when they buy a product that doesn't work. Hi! Since I've seen the results that we generate for people so consistently, no. That you're actually wasting time trying to do it on your own. You have a much higher probability to fail going out there and trying it yourself. You have a much higher probability to get the result faster and get the result when you buy it. What if I told you that if you spend a little money right now, you'd have a higher probability to buy it because you actually invested into the outcome. You're here right now for free. For you to go out there and take this seriously. You have to obviously be really sold on the outcome potential. If you actually bought right now. How much more serious would you take getting that outcome? Because you already spent a little bit of money. You got to get that money back. makes you take things more seriously. You know what would make you more intentional through the education process? Spending some money. OK, I want the people who want to buy speed, who want to buy support, who want to make that investment to take the result and outcome more seriously, who want to essentially buy being intentional, okay? Go ahead and purchase that product right now. Links in the description. Over here, press that button. Music. So it's like. I'm just going back. I'm pitching differently now. Okay, the first pitch I have to obviously introduce the product. I have to introduce what it is. I have to talk about the price. I have to talk about some common questions. It's really just like showing them the stuff. You know, and because it's only two to five minutes, I don't get a lot of time to yap about. And then I got to get right into the Q &A and back to the content. The second pitch. If you notice, on this little green, this little green retention graph. Okay, when you look right here, when I'm doing the second pitch. There should be about 40 to upwards of 60% of your audience that you got there in the first place still there at this point. This is a very undermined tactic. Of doing three pitches. To a cold audience in a webinar. Okay. All I'm trying to do here is essentially do almost like an extended pitch. So like what I left out in the first part of the pitch. I can now talk about in the second pitch here. I don't need to say the same exact shit that I did in the first one. I don't need to reiterate all the stuff that comes with it. Notice what I provided you into the example is more like framing stuff around why you'd buy. Okay, the third pitch, these are obviously the people who have the biggest skepticism. You know? Or are leechers, which are just there who aren't going to buy anything anyway. They just want value, which is fine. You're always going to get those. But that third pitch, that third pitch is much deeper. Right. I get to do a longer pitch. So I'm essentially doing all three. I'm pitching the offer again. I'm talking about what comes with it. I'm doing the framing stuff and I'm doing objection handling. Okay, I prefer to start the third pitch with the objection handling. Then I go through what comes with it again. And then I go through those framing things. And then I do Q &A to do live objection handling. Okay, those are the differences between the three pitches. The differences in content. Okay, look down here to the bottom left again. The differences in content are very simple to understand. Surface level, deeper, deepest. Okay. So as I talked about, I can give people what it is and how it works. Second part, I can give them examples of how it works. Third part. I might even start helping them like an exercise to like put it into action. or to start trying it or some kind of test. You know, one of my favorite examples of this. We had a guy doing webinars for a high-ticket product for red light panels. The product was $5,000 and it was this bundle of a few different red light panels that the guy sold. And he had this physical test. That you could do with your body, which he would do for that final third. And he would demonstrate to somebody through a physical test how red light could benefit him in a few ways. He'd ask people, he'd be like, ' anybody wearing gold?' and then somebody who's wearing gold would be like, 'Yeah, I'm wearing gold' and he would he would have them become like the full screen and he'd be like, 'Take your ring, swipe it across your forehead.' And if a little black line shows up, that means you're low on iron. But you know what? You might not even have to supplement iron. In order to solve that problem. And then he'd go into this whole spiel about how red light panels can actually help with. Some of these things that people would otherwise externally supplement, he would then have people do little things like: 'Who here has arthritis? Like, do me a favor— if you guys just bend your wrists and like rotate them like this. Does anybody have any problems doing that? Does it hurt a little bit? Do you have as much flexibility as you did when you were a kid, you know? And then, naturally, somebody on the call will be like, 'Yeah, no, it hurts.' And then he'll be like, 'Red light solves that too.' There's actually a, and then he pulls up this frequency chart. And the frequency chart of red light changes what the red light solves. So, as an example, there's a frequency for bone density. It's a frequency for increasing your vascularity and helping with vein recovery, blood flow, circulation. There's a frequency that helps. wisdom. Tissue. related to joints such as ligaments, tendons, connective tissue. There's a frequency for your skin. as in just the most surface level part of your body that it can hit. There's red light panels that target your nervous system. OK, so he would just give like four examples. And there are physical examples. OK, for as an example, like an info business or some type of high ticket service, that third part of the webinar, that final third, is more so geared towards. Like getting them into motion. Okay, that's the biggest thing that most people miss. They don't actually get people into the flow of doing it. Okay. Look at it like this. If I get people to start. What's the probability of those people buying from me when they realize how much help they need in doing it? But I'm trying to sell them something. Where I help them do it instead of them doing it, if I get them to start doing it, they'll quickly realize how fucked they're going to be if they try to do it on their own. You see? That's the key here in that final third. That last bit of depth, obviously, it makes no sense without the first and second section. You see what I mean? And then obviously I'm going into my pitch and some final Q &A, which is really objection handling at the end there. Okay, now I want to give. A quick moment. To just finish off this cold section before we pivot to warm, and I'll do some questions in between, okay? Just let me close this up real quick. In the sections of the webinar, the first, second, and third pitch. Okay. You should have somebody at the webinar. That you intentionally placed there to plant questions that you want to strategically answer at those stages of pitching. Okay, so as an example. If I had the first third pitch where I introduced, like, what comes with it, what it is, and the price. Ideally, I could get somebody to plant a question that's like. Who's this best for? Who shouldn't buy it? It's a great first question. The second pitch. Where, like I said, I'm doing more of like a framing pitch, you know, trying to tell people to buy speed, buy the support, buy the outcome, invest in order to get the outcome. Talk about what's your odds of getting the outcome without buying. Probably pretty low, you know, and not just because you won't get the information, but because you won't take it seriously, you know. Ideally, same logic. I have somebody there that plants some shit in the chat that's like— damn, that makes a lot of sense. You know, I didn't think of it that way. And then you get to say some snazzy shit like, 'That's right, man.' Yeah, people who pay, they pay attention. They take it seriously. And you can give examples. You want to give a lot of examples. If you guys notice in my communication, I generally use a lot of analogies and metaphors. Okay. I'll consistently talk about financial loss or financial gain and some type of investment. That can give a higher probability for an outcome. I could come up with any fucking random example. I could be like, man, imagine you wanted to start becoming like a professional e-sports player. You wanted to be one of those pro gamers. Who gets to sit at home and stream and like, you know, get paid to play video games. Where do you think the best place is to start? It's like for me, I would start investing right away in as much equipment as possible. I'd want the best desk, the best chair, you know, the best environment to play the games and the best cameras. You know, I'd want to make enough of an investment at the beginning. To make me take it seriously, because there's the most friction at the beginning of a process. If I want to be a pro gamer and I have like a basic laptop and the fucking webcam on the laptop with the built-in laptop mic, I'm going to feel like shit when I go to do it. I don't want to feel like shit when I go to do it. The hardest part is at the beginning. That's where the most friction is. That's where the most people fail is at the very beginning of the process. If I invest more money than most people at the beginning, I don't want to lose money. I don't know about you guys. Who here wants to lose money? You know, not me. But if I invest enough, okay? As as shocking as it might sound, I actually have a higher probability to make more money. Because I don't know about you guys, but I care about getting the money back that I spend on some kind of business-related investment. I can tell the same information to you that I am right now after you buy from me. You'll retain more of the information after you buy from me. Because you bought. You see, it's like, that's that whole second pitch too. It's like framing like that. Okay. And like I said, the final third is more of a kind of like objection handling pitch. So it's like you'd be like, 'All right, now listen, we've gone through all the content at this point. Why wouldn't you buy?' Let me go through some of the most common reasons that we hear from people going through our sales process. Reason number one. And then it's like, 'I'm just going to start fucking racking them off.' And then I'm going to let some... What naturally happens when you're doing this as well, by the way, is you'll accumulate some of these questions in the chat anyway. But as I talked about, pro tip, you drop. Some strategic objections into that part of the pitch anyway from somebody that you planted in the audience. Okay, pretty simple, simple stuff. Okay, now, long story short. You guys should be able to unmute yourselves at this point. And ask any questions that you need. You can also drop any questions in the chat if you'd like. Go ahead and ask away if you've got something. And if not, drop a question in the chat. Go ahead, Tim. So quick question. Obviously, for us, we'd be sending people to book a call 15k offer, so wouldn't want to do that directly. Just check out the link. Do you still send them through like the regular application process, or do you just send them like directly to book a call? Great question. We still like to do an application process, and here's why. We will do what's called app grading, and the people who have the highest score will be allowed to book a priority calendar slot, meaning they'll be able to book soonest, closest to the webinar itself. The people who have the lowest score, we're going to push them out all the way up to about seven to 10 days out, depending on the volume of people we have at the webinar itself. In addition to that, we're going to pixel the highest quality people with the standard event of schedule. And we're going to allow that data to accumulate on the pixel, the webinar pixel behind the scenes. Hang on one second. Let me drink this water. We're going to allow that data to accumulate on the pixel behind the scenes. Because eventually, instead of optimizing our webinars, for the front end opt-in using the standard event of lead or complete registration, we're going to be able to optimize our events for a delayed pixel fire using schedule from all those qualified highest app graded people. Does that make sense? Yeah, it does. So I assume there's some Zapier tech shit on the back end where you can just send the upgrade back to the Pixel. Yes, just the conversion ABI. Nothing complicated at all. Got it. And then you mentioned seven to 10 days out. I assume, does that mean like... when you're doing a webinar, just i mean this is gonna be kind of like tactical stuff? But are you pausing your call funnel ads or are they running simultaneously? Depends on the sales team and the volume that they can handle. Most teams, when we first start, do not have two teams for both funnels. They'll have one team that works across both funnels. And that will require us to essentially throttle or turn off completely the daily lead flow ads, which are like call funnels. DM strategies. Um, things like that, and then obviously all the flow will go to the webinar. And then once that volume starts to die, you do a little flip-flop, flick them back on. Um, okay. The other instance is we have a sales team that has a dedicated team to both. And this really depends on the frequency that you do webinars. If you do a webinar as described weekly for cold, and then monthly for warm, you won't be able to do daily lead flow for the call funnels and for the DM strategies because you should be booking out your calendars enough to have sustained flow with that. Now, that's not good. Because then we'll obviously lose out on all the additional revenue that can come from those other daily lead flow funnels. At that point, usually what the best sales teams will do is they'll have teams for both. They'll have a team that only does daily lead flow. They have a team that only does the webinars. And then in that case, obviously we don't have to throttle anything. We're in perpetual scale mode on both. OK, that makes perfect sense. And then one last question in terms of promoting the cold. Because if you're doing five webinars a month, right, which is it seems like it makes sense. How are you like, how far out are you promoting it? And then, are you doing like new creatives every week of like, 'Hey, you know, April 24th, I'm doing a webinar.' On April 31st, I'm doing a webinar. It's like a brand new ad or like what's the best practice to do that? No, no, we don't do anything like that. For our webbies, okay, for the perpetual cold ones, like what we'll do looks more like these. Okay, where we have our creative that pushes to the webinar, but you'll notice like there's no dates. There's no dates, copy. There's no dates in the headlines. It just talks about like a training that's coming up. Okay. All that changes is. The link itself, like, let me be clear when I say that, a little more clear. The URL does not change. OK, just as soon as like the literal moment that the webinar starts. For the current one, the page itself will be updated to start promoting the next webinar. But the URL does not change, just the content on the page and what the page links to for the next webinar they register for is what changes. The ads themselves are more like evergreen. There's no content in the webinar. I'm sorry, there's no content about the webinar dates. or the day even. It's just, hey, go apply for this training. will register for this training. And by the way, we never called a webinar. Okay. We call it a training. Or we call it a workshop. Okay. Sometimes we even call it a masterclass. Never use the word webinar. Okay. Other questions? Are you guys just running them on Zoom? Of course. Cool. Hey, Jeremy, I had a quick question about segmenting the warm and co-leads. I started these last week, doing two a week. And pretty much what I was thinking about co-versus warm is just anyone that didn't attend the webinar. So I was just segmenting by if you didn't hit our thank you page. I was excluding those. I'm trying to see the. the purpose of excluding anyone in like who's engaged on your websites, who's engaged with you on Instagram. It's not true. Yeah. It's not true. If you don't exclude the warm audiences, warm audiences are not what you just self-defined it as. I am not self-defining warm audiences. I am telling you factually, warm audiences are website visitors, customer lists, email lists. All the social interaction and all your video viewers, those should all be excluded in order to actually get true cold audience webinars accomplished. Otherwise, you could do whatever targeting you want and with advantage detailed targeting and advantage audience controls, just by default turned on for a majority of accounts nowadays, it's still going to go after the people that are in your warm audiences if they are not directly excluded. Okay, so you are not actually running a cold webinar. And the reason that we have to run true cold webinars and that we get the opportunity to do them at a higher frequency in the first place is because they're truly cold. If we perpetually do this frequency to warm audiences, you're gonna fatigue this strategy faster than you could fucking blink. OK, it will not be able to scale and it will not be something that has legs. OK, it will be something you could run for weeks and then your lead cost will dramatically climb. It will not be, because of ad fatigue. It'll be, because of conversion mechanism fatigue. Again, we have to do a warm audience, ideally once a month. We can do twice a month if it's a different offer in the second one. Okay, to warm audiences, but once a month, to warm and we can do it really like I just described the client I just showed you by the way who's in the inner circle with us here. They do twice a week. Okay, the cold. Got you. Can you give that list one more time? Just writing it down. Real quick. Can I what? The list you said defines warm. I just want to write it down, make sure I got it. Yeah, okay, long story short, website visitors. Got it. Email list. Obviously, customer list. All the social interaction so you get to go on to Facebook and Instagram there and your audience controls people who saved a post, messaged the page, people who clicked on an ad, people who engaged with a post, people who engaged with the profile, people who follow or like the page, people who visited the profile, all those get excluded. And then video viewers, all those get excluded as well. Those are all warm audiences. Got it. Thank you. No problem. And ideally, just to be clear on time frames, for those you can do all the social ones for up to 365 days. You can do the website visitors for 180 days. You can do lead form interaction for up to 90 days. You do video views for up to 365 days, and obviously, customer lists and email lists are in perpetuity— no time frame on those. All right. Very good. Any other questions before we continue to the warm audience best practices? If you're if you're constantly excluding anyone that's like interacted with you, do you not eventually just run into extremely cold traffic that doesn't convert? Well, it depends on audience size, to be fair, for your addressable cold markets. There are, you know, to be fair to some of you in here, like. 10 000 person audiences is your entire addressable market, you know, like Chris and Chad are examples of that, where they sell the $20 million plus B2B businesses in the medical space. So their addressable market's very small. They'd either be running smaller webinars or they would just not do them at this high of a frequency. If you have a larger addressable market, then obviously you can pump this out at a much higher pace and rate. And, you know, you won't run into that ceiling. So to your point, yes, it is very dependent on audience size that dictates the potential. Got it. Cool. Makes sense. Other questions? Boom. That we carry on. Do do do do do do. All right, so warm audiences. Come on. Okay. So warm's a little different. Okay. When we do warm. First of all, You. BING! This is very key. You think that they know. And... like you. Most don't. Okay, so let me give you a quick example. I remember one time we had a client. Only targeting warm audiences. Okay. And warm audiences, to them, meant website visitors. All the social interaction I just described— email lists, but no video views. Okay, not a single video of you is included in this. And You know, they were doing great until they started to try to scale. Once they started to try to scale. They just... We call it spinning the wheels, right? If you see a car that ever gets stuck in like mud or, I used to live on the beach and people could drive on the beach over there and, like, cars would get stuck all the time. And, like, when they try to drive, it would just dig them deeper into the ground. It wouldn't move anywhere. That's called spinning your wheels. So whenever they would try to spend more money, their cost per lead would just climb. And it's, you know, they needed to expand their audiences. So I came in and I was like, 'Oh, well, hey, you're not targeting video viewers.' And this guy, he had a fucking ton of video viewers that he could target. More video viewers, believe it or not, than social interaction and website visitors. So I was like, 'Hey man, like, why don't we just try that?' So we created a 25% video view audience, which had a few million people in it, and right away added a few million people to the audience. And it almost felt like The following webinar was cold. Okay. It just. I didn't. Didn't. have the same type of people in it. They were completely different. They were far less warmed up. They had no idea who the guy was. and this comes from the fact that, like and I'm sure you guys realize this, like. If I go through just some of my, like, group chats with my friends, they send me all kinds of random ass videos from people. Sometimes we're like making fun of people, you know, sometimes they're just like silly videos. You know, I don't follow the page. I don't know who the fuck they are. I have no idea of their background or their story, you know, and I might have watched the entirety of the video. You know what I mean? I'll give you a great one. Yesterday, there was a guy, he had a video that Brandon Carter had shared with me. And he was like, he was like wearing a hat with some podcast equipment. And like, it was all dark. And he was like. I asked God why. He keeps. Let's just listen to it together, actually. It's hilarious to listen to out loud. And it's far better than what I could ever depict, you know? I'm just not that cringy like this guy. And, uh... Yeah. All right, here we go. Yes, God. Why are you putting me through rough waters? Nobody replied. Because your enemy is capturing you. I asked God. Why are you putting me through tough waters? And he said, 'cause your enemies can't swim. You know, and I watched a hundred percent of that video. It's fucking hilarious. Okay. But like not in a good way. 어? We could still see your iPad notes, I couldn't see the video. Yeah, I didn't show you the video. I let you hear the video. Okay, I'm putting you all on mute again. Don't my flow up. So long story short. That guy, right? I watched 100% of that video. Okay, but it's like, do I like that guy? No, no, I don't fucking like that. Like, we were making fun of the guy. You know what I mean? And it's like, the point I'm trying to make is, if that guy retargets a video viewer. And we see him on a webinar. Put in the chat, are we warm or cold in that example? Right? Are we a warm audience or a cold audience? He could have targeted a 100% video viewer. I think Brandon and I both watched it like two to three times all the way through for sure. Okay. And again, We're not warm. Not even close. See what I'm saying? So anyway, moral of the story is: video viewers, you've got to kind of be careful with because when you include video viewers, it's going to expand the audience a lot. But it's going to cause issues for like lowering the overall warmth of your webinars. If you want... Like. Truly. the warmest people. These are people who follow you, who like your page. You know, who interact with content. Video viewers, believe it or not, although yes, they're technically warm audiences, like I just described, they can lower the... They can lower how hot it is from being like a nice big fire to like just war members. You know what I mean? So anyway, moral of the story is. We have a lot of the same practices. For cold webinars that we would for warm too. Okay, it's just a little different. So. Again, the things that carry over. Let me just copy them down here so we have it. First of all, We still jump straight into it. We still want to make sure that we stupid shit. Let me duplicate this so it's easy for us here. Duplicate. Bang. Look at that, huh? Okay, there we go. So we still want to jump straight into it. We still want to do value-dense content. Believe it or not, you can still get right around the same show rate as you otherwise would. The difference is, guys, they're just not cruel. And they're just not like. They're not as shitty to you, you know? They're at least like a little more respectful in that regard. But they're not. It's not like they love you yet. You know, that's the biggest thing that I think a lot of people mistake. When they do some type of warm webinar, they show up like it's like they're subscribers on YouTube or something, you know, or like, it's like somebody watching their story, right? There's different layers of warmth. It's like when I post on my story, it's like for almost all intents and purposes, that's arguably probably one of the warmest audiences that I had. I'd say the same with YouTube subscribers for the most part. Like that's like a very warm audience, you know? They might not know me to the degree that the people who follow my stories do. But you see, it starts to like track outward. OK, then there's people who follow me. And as you guys know, from an algorithm perspective on social now. How many people actually watch all your shit out of everybody who follows you already? You know, not everybody, that's for sure. But when you do warm audience ads, like for the first time in a long time, you're finally going to reach them. You know? So like I described, like as soon as I start even targeting followers, it's like I'm not technically targeting people who have that same warmth. As a YouTube subscriber or as a story viewer. You see what I mean? So it's like all these general lessons still apply in a lot of ways. To this. When we do our warm webinars, okay? The difference is, is they're generally a little more receptive and the retention's generally a little stronger. Not always. But for the most part, okay? So when we look... And as an example, like our retention graph, it still generally looks like this. Okay, still is still is pretty much this right here. So I might just have a slightly different green line. For my retention on this whole thing. Like, instead of this steep of a drop, I might get people to stick a little longer with me, but not much. Not much, okay, just a little longer than cold comparatively where it's a little steeper. From here, same logic applies with the pitch strategy. Same logic applies with the three different sections of the webinar and how I'm going to flow through it. Also, length. We talk about in depth in the Mastering Webinars SOP. Affluent Webinars, 90 minutes max. Uh, general public webinars can be upwards of three hours long. General public generally has far, far less risk. That they're willing to take. So they require more hand-holding, more coddling. Comparatively to affluent audiences. Affluent audiences. They are going to be. Much more direct and they're going to have a preference for speed. Higher level information. Remember... I'll give you guys a great example. I was on a consulting deal one time with the Harmon brothers. And the Harmon Brothers had hired me. They flew me out to Provo, Utah. And they had an Airbnb house that they had rented for two days for them and their staff to come into and for me to do this. This, you know, consulting deal in. What I did with them is I talked to them about all these content ad strategies. And at the time, this was years and years ago, I think we had talked about the Venus flytrap. We had talked about the harvester. And I believe I also talked to them about the Forester. And I only needed about a day and a half to get through all the information. But respectfully, to them, you know, they paid for two days. So for the second half of the day on day two, I was like, 'Hey, guys, you know, like we're pretty much done now. We worked through everything that we needed to do. You know, like you guys got all your ideas.' You're ready to go film. We can either cut it short or I just kind of got some other random stuff that I can talk to you guys about. They were like, 'Yeah, like, you know, let's just pick through some random stuff.' Like, what do you got? And I was like, 'Well, we. You know, got some of these YouTube best practices currently.' And they were like, 'Yeah, surely let's go through that.' Now to be clear, when I'm talking about the content ad strategies with them, I'm talking about things at a high level. Like a very high level. Strategy, that's the thing. Like when you're talking to rich, affluent people, as soon as you get into the weeds of the tactics. They don't give a shit like their eyes that's not for them that's for their status. You know, so like they'll tolerate listening to that because their staff were in the room. But it's like the main things that they cared about, like the actual Harmon brothers cared about, were the big, like the idea part of it, how it worked at a high level. You lose the rich people when you go into the tactics. OK, the general public, though. They love the tactics. Okay, the poorer somebody is. And to be fair to you guys, this is also important to note, like you can still be affluent, but like in the trenches doing something that's like some type of skill and obviously still care about the tactics of it. But on a webinar, it's very important to understand. Usually, it's better for affluent demographics to stay at the highest level, especially for that first part. That's why we do the thirds the way we do. The first third is meant to stay at like the idea level of it all. The big idea level of it all, the strategy level of it all. Section two can get into some of the tactics, but it's like, remember what I used for section two is a lot of examples. Of the strategy and action, okay? And in section three, if you noticed what I did, I had said, 'get them into action,' like get them to start doing something, get them to do exercise, like get them officially into the game. At no point did I say, 'Switch from strategy into deep tactics.' Okay? What you do, like I described, is you have a very high-level conversation with affluent audiences. The general public, to be fair, you typically still keep the first section at a high level. But you pepper in some tactics. The general public thinks you're absolutely full of shit. If you do not talk tactically to them too. OK, the higher level people, they're just going to leave if you start talking tactics because they don't give a fuck about the tech. They're going to pay you and get somebody else to do this in most instances. Or if they understand it themselves, it's really just so they can hold the person they're going to have do it accountable. Or, in that rare instance, it's because they're going to do it themselves. So, anyway. Very important to note. The only real difference, to be quite direct with you guys, on warm audiences versus cold is you do just get... a little bit more leeway, like you get a little more leash. Um, to kind of pepper in more of like your personality right. In cold, I do not recommend adding what we call like character content. Okay. In warm audiences, like as an example, what I'm doing here right now, and like Timothy asks a question or like interrupts my flow about some, I'm just going to kind of jokingly be like, 'Oh, I'm muting all you guys.' Like don't interrupt my flow, you know? If that was somebody who literally has no idea who I am and doesn't know that I'm like, just kind of like joking and like taking a jab at Timothy, you know? It's like, they're going to kind of be offended by it. Let's just be like, 'FUCK THIS GUY.' And they're going to leave. You see, it's like that's called character content. Like character content is like just some of the being yourself kind of stuff. In warm audiences, I actually do recommend that you pepper in more of that, like just be your truest self in your warm audiences, you know, like it's okay to talk about some. Like kind of personal stuff or just, you know, again, just be a little bit more of who you actually are. I want to be really clear when I say this, okay? Because to some of you that might come off as confusing or unclear. You constantly see me and experience me. As like who I am, you know, like I. I say things like, you know, I don't say the word 'retard' or 'gay' on YouTube, as an example. Like, I don't tweet the word 'retard' or 'gay'; it's like. But you know, I say those words in my life, so it's like, long story short. Like, I say I'm around you guys because, like, I said, you guys are like experiencing my truest self. You know. Like, when you guys come to my house and you come to the masterminds, like, obviously, you're literally in my home. It's like, you get to meet my fiancé. You get to meet my little wiener dogs. If you were lucky to see them on Sunday, you know, it's like. Just little things like that. The cold audiences. It's like, again, the clearest difference, if you really don't understand what I'm saying here, is like, just go watch my YouTube, you know, go watch like me on a podcast or something. It's like, dude— I'm not peppering in any of that. It is just value-dense, straight to the fucking point. I can help you with these things. You only really get the exposure to like more of the character content on shorts, reels, TikToks. That's where that's for. That's what we call it— character development content. And you can pepper that in a little bit on warm webinars. It does help, believe it or not. So anyway, those are the biggest differences. There's not much other than that. But to be clear, just to reiterate the frequency. You could do a weekly or even up to a twice a week cold audience if you have the... The audience size for it. And you could do an ideally once a month warm audience webinar. So you do not saturate your ability to continue running that funnel. If you try to do it at too high of a frequency, it generally fails. Okay, cool. Allow me to. You guys should be able to unmute yourselves now if you guys have any follow-up questions. You can also drop any questions in the chat if you have any. What do you got, if anything? I have a question. So my thought was: I haven't done a webinar yet or I did. It was like two, two and a half years ago. It's been a while. So my thought was. Like, do the warm first, you know, squeeze all the life out of that, and then from there start doing the colds weekly. Would you agree that would be, like, the best way to do it? So if you've never done webinars before, or if you are— like new to it or it's been a long time since you've done a webinar—I completely agree with what you just described. Get yourself just warmed up to doing it, you know? Um, don't do it at too high of a frequency, like, just. Get yourself the exposure to acclimating to the skill of doing webinars again or for the first time ever. And to your point. Then take the risk. It's kind of like if you ski. You have the green, the blue diamonds, and the black diamonds. It's like the greens are always the beginner hills. They're the easiest to ski down. And honestly, man, if it's been a while since I've like right now, I don't think I've skied since before COVID. But I used to ski a lot. It's like I'm not a bad skier, but it's been a long time, man. So if I went skiing again, I would absolutely start on the green hills first. The green hills meaning that's your warm audience. Those are the beginner things. You can even go live on Instagram as an example. Just as like kind of warming yourself up to being live again and what it's like. And. To your point, like once you officially get, you know, cracked back into it and you're feeling good— that's when you can elevate to the blue diamonds, which is like the medium tier difficulty. And then eventually even black diamonds. So blue and black are more of the cold audiences. The green would be considered like warm audiences for sure. So it's a great way to kind of get back into it. I'd agree with that. And how long would you promote the warm webinar before doing it? I thought it was like maybe a week, week and a half. I don't know, like. We like to be 14 days out. We always like to promote 14 days out. The only time that we don't have that luxury is obviously when we're doing cold. It's seven days out at its maximum. Got it. And if we do twice a week webinars, then we don't even get seven days. We technically get three and a half days. Got it. Let me disclose this real quick. The risks of having less time all really come down to the fact that you're going to be kind of fucked. If something happens. You know, like you have ads that might not perform or you didn't film new ads and your ads are fatiguing. Maybe something like timeframe-related happens, like Easter this weekend, as an example. It's like these things can all kind of fuck you. And if you have less time. To promote your webinar, you're going to have less people at higher costs. If you have more time, that 14-day example, or even seven days once you start cracking weekly. That's gonna you're gonna appreciate that time if and when something happens that you need to fix. You know, otherwise you're gonna lose a lot of uh, a lot of momentum and speed that you would otherwise capture. You know. Got it. And I mean, obviously, all the leads that come in in the 14 days, cause I'm also running a call funnel. There's no reason they can't just buy from the call funnel in the meantime. Yeah, that's fair and true. Yes. Yes. Awesome. Appreciate it. Okay, cool. Vince asks, 'usually ramps spend more and more the closer to Webby? No, Vince, we generally keep a lifetime budget on webinar campaigns unless we get to the point where we're doing them weekly. So, like, if I'm doing a warm audience campaign. I'm going to do a lifetime budget. And I'm going to start promoting it about 14 days out. So there's no real. like difference in spend throughout that period of time. It's really up to the algorithm there. And then, if I'm doing a perpetual weekly, then it's just essentially a daily budget campaign. To your point, yes, I might technically be spending more money at different times closer to a webinar. But it's not actually because I want to spend more closer to the webinar. It's just like, look, I'm just kind of scaling it anyway, since it's a profitable kind of thing. You know, I'm not intentionally trying to just spend more money closer to the webinar. The logic of that usually like when you're trying to like think with what you asked is. People show up more when they register closer. I'm not trying to bullshit you here just because I constantly talk about like framing stuff pre-webinar, like pre-call funnels, but we generally see a better person show up and we get a slightly higher show rate from the people that are about seven days out. Because there's more time for them to go through the hammer them content. There's more time to get those value-dense emails. There's just more time to anticipate it. You know, I'll give you a really good example. So in September, Brandon Carter and I were so excited for Dua Lipa to come to the Heat Arena right here across the street. We've known about it since sometime last year when we saw it promoted on the side of the arena there. And Oh boy, you know, just the anticipation. There's two concerts in a row. I might go to both, you know, Dua Lipa is awesome. However, imagine it's the day of the actual concert and I didn't know about it until the day of the concert. I wouldn't have built the excitement. You know, I wouldn't have like randomly listened to her music between when I first became aware in the concert itself. I wouldn't have talked about it like I am here, you know? It's like there's excitement and anticipation that can build. And there's a lot of pre-framing that can happen too. Prior to the event itself, that when it's just like a day away or the day of, you know, or even just a few days out, it's like, I just don't have as much of that, like anticipation that can build up. So when you do the framing, right. And you do the value-dense emails, right. And you do the confirmation pages, right. You can really play into that, like anticipation and excitement. When done correctly. If you just do it like the day or two before. You'd think, yeah, that's the highest probability for the person to show up, but. It hasn't been my experience recently, surprisingly. I've seen consumer behavior change a lot in that regard. There's a lot of like long-form consumption that's generally required nowadays to like actually have high show rates. Rather than how it used to be. So anyway, that's how we think of it. Okay, other questions? You should be able to unmute yourselves. Or you can drop a question in the chat. Up to you. Give you a couple seconds to type it out if you got anything or unmute. Are you varying the presentation? Because I know obviously you said there's like slight tweaks in how you start the webinar and how you approach it with the warm versus the cold. But is it like a different training? Are you training on the same thing? Over and over and over. We're trained pretty much on the same thing. We don't really vary the content itself too much. Okay. The only... The only variance of that would be depending on, like, if I isolate my warm audience to like a very specific subset of my warm audience. Like we'd call that like a micro webinar, you know, which is like, okay, all the people who went through like this, you know, thing like this lead magnet or like bought this, you know, like we have some clients as an example, they get 10,000 book buyers in a month. And, you know, we'll run a webinar specific to just the book buyers. And if that's the case, you know, we'll talk a lot about the content of the book, but we would never do that for like somebody who has no idea what the fuck the book is for the other webinars that happen in the month. You know what I mean? So that'd be the only instance of that. But it's such a— There's not like a concern of, oh, they saw my cold webinar and then they became warm and then they saw the same thing. That's not like a concern at all. No. So we have a good example of this is Cody Sanchez. Like we see about one third of her total buyers watch at least two webinars before actually buying. And those webinars are the same. They're just done monthly. Okay, got it. Yeah. Interesting. Faith asks, do you require registration via Zoom? Yeah. So what we do, Faith, we have. Typically Zapier connected to whatever the opt-in form is. So, like, if we're using like ClickFunnels or GoHighLevel or HubSpot, we'll just have a Zapier integration on the backend to register that person into the Zoom webinar. But yes, we're always registering the person into the Zoom webinar. Yes. You guys do recorded replays. I hate... I hate recorded replays. Uh, they're just not working that well anymore. You know, they get a lot less people from what we've seen replays, anything recorded, anything like evergreen. It's just not working nearly as well right now. We're seeing live as the main proponent of lots of money coming from webinars. Anytime we try to do something different, we're just not seeing the result that we want. So, no, not really, no. I will say this. If you look in the mastering webinars, SOP. you'll see a section for the follow-up of the webinar. We do this thing called a pop-up event, which is essentially just a smaller live webinar with different content. And typically, we'll still, in the emails, promote like a replay page. that somebody could go to and watch it. but, as I described, we're just not seeing a lot of money from it so we'll technically do it, but you know. not a lot of revenue that comes from it nowadays. Faith says, what are your thoughts on having a webinar for qualified leads and another webinar for unqualified leads where you pitch a low ticket? Yeah, I wouldn't add that much friction, Faith. I would never do something for the unqualified people. I would just try to drop something into something to help liquidate the ad and spend the bottom there. I don't build businesses around unqualified people. I build businesses around the qualified ones. Okay, other questions? Cool. Oh, we got one more. Tyler says, always one CTA. Sometimes they'll do more of like a—they'll frame it like, 'Hey, go fill out this quiz to see which one of our offers might be best for you.' But we don't have that. That's a minority by far. It's not even close to a majority. Majority of our clients just pitch one main thing and it's always the highest ticket thing that they have to sell that's scalable. All right, cool.
